does: This paper introduces COSMO-RL, a mixed reinforcement learning framework designed to train large multimodal reasoning models that balance safety and capability simultaneously. The approach uses multiobjective optimization to jointly optimize for safety, instruction following, and reasoning performance, addressing the problem that single-objective training causes either over-refusal on benign inputs or unsafe compliance on risky ones. The authors release COSMO-R1, a resulting model that demonstrates improved safety against multimodal jailbreaks—exploits combining images and text to bypass guardrails—while maintaining or enhancing performance on multimodal reasoning and instruction-following tasks. Empirical results show the framework reduces unnecessary refusals, transfers effectively across different model backbones, and ablations validate the core design choices for jointly advancing both safety and general capability.
connects: This work builds on the emerging challenge of aligning large multimodal models, extending prior safety alignment research from language-only models to the multimodal domain where adversaries can exploit the interaction between vision and text modalities. It responds to limitations of existing single-objective alignment approaches, which often suffer from policy drift and create a false trade-off between safety and utility. The paper draws on reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) and preference-based alignment techniques, adapting them to handle multiple competing objectives through mixed RL formulations. It connects to research on jailbreaking detection and robustness in multimodal systems, and extends recent work on reasoning model alignment by introducing multiobjective RL as a mechanism to prevent the capability degradation that typically accompanies safety-focused fine-tuning.
enables: This framework opens pathways for developing multimodal AI systems that can safely operate in real-world applications by demonstrating that safety and reasoning capability need not be at odds. The multiobjective RL approach provides a general template for balancing multiple desiderata in large models, potentially applicable to other alignment challenges beyond safety. The work enables future research into more sophisticated multimodal jailbreak detection and prevention, as well as development of better safety benchmarks for reasoning-oriented multimodal models. By showing that the framework transfers across backbones, it suggests a pathway toward scaling trustworthy multimodal reasoning to larger, more capable models. The reduction of over-refusal opens room for improving human-AI collaboration, and the stability of the training pipeline points toward more reliable deployment of multimodal models in safety-critical domains.
